metadata.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

one_liner.via: PagedAttention and block-level KV cache memory management
one_liner.because: eliminates memory fragmentation and enables flexible KV cache sharing across requests and decoding algorithms

claim_tree.statement: Existing LLM serving systems cannot batch many requests because each request’s KV cache is large, dynamic, and suffers from fragmentation and redundant duplication, limiting batch size.

children.statement: A fused kernel that performs reshape and block write of the KV cache in every Transformer layer, splitting the cache into blocks, reshaping them to a memory layout optimized for block read, and writing them to positions specified by the block table, thereby minimizing kernel launch overheads.

claim.statement: A fused kernel that performs reshape and block write of the KV cache in every Transformer layer, splitting the cache into blocks, reshaping them to a memory layout optimized for block read, and writing them to positions specified by the block table, thereby minimizing kernel launch overheads.
entity_names: Fused reshape and block write kernel

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: separate reshape and block write kernels
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Reduced kernel launch overhead at cost of increased kernel complexity

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache block -> GPU memory location via fused reshape and block write kernel
systems_facets.s5_policy: fuse reshape and block write into single kernel
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: CUDA-capable GPU with warp-level memory coalescing

children.statement: A fused kernel that adapts the FasterTransformer attention kernel to read KV cache according to the block table and perform attention operations on the fly, assigning a GPU warp to read each block for coalesced memory access and adding support for variable sequence lengths within a request batch.

claim.statement: A fused kernel that adapts the FasterTransformer attention kernel to read KV cache according to the block table and perform attention operations on the fly, assigning a GPU warp to read each block for coalesced memory access and adding support for variable sequence lengths within a request batch.
entity_names: Fused block read and attention kernel

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Higher kernel complexity for better memory coalescing and variable-length handling

algorithm_facets.g5: KV cache blocks and block table

children.statement: PagedAttention leads to 20–26% higher attention kernel latency compared to FasterTransformer.

A fused kernel that batches block copy operations issued by the copy‑on‑write mechanism, which may involve discontinuous blocks, into a single kernel launch to mitigate the overhead of numerous small cudaMemcpyAsync calls.

claim.statement: A fused kernel that batches block copy operations issued by the copy‑on‑write mechanism, which may involve discontinuous blocks, into a single kernel launch to mitigate the overhead of numerous small cudaMemcpyAsync calls.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Reduces copy overhead, increases kernel complexity
universal_facets.analogy_source: Consolidating many small deliveries into one truck

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Block copy operations -> GPU memory via fused kernel launch
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: CUDA-capable GPU with support for asynchronous memory copy and kernel launches

children.statement: vLLM implements a fork method that creates a new sequence from an existing one.

claim.statement: vLLM implements a fork method that creates a new sequence from an existing one.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: sequence -> KV cache slots via fork/append/free
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with KV cache memory

children.statement: vLLM implements an append method that appends a new token to an existing sequence.

claim.statement: vLLM implements an append method that appends a new token to an existing sequence.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: sequence -> token buffer via append
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: vLLM implements a free method that deletes a sequence.

claim.statement: vLLM implements a free method that deletes a sequence.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: sequence -> GPU memory buffer via free method
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU

children.statement: In parallel sampling, vLLM creates multiple output sequences from a single input sequence using fork, appends new tokens each iteration with append, and deletes sequences that meet a stopping condition using free.

claim.statement: In parallel sampling, vLLM creates multiple output sequences from a single input sequence using fork, appends new tokens each iteration with append, and deletes sequences that meet a stopping condition using free.

children.statement: vLLM applies the fork-append-free strategy in beam search and prefix sharing.

claim.statement: vLLM applies the fork-append-free strategy in beam search and prefix sharing.

children.statement: vLLM believes future decoding algorithms can be supported by combining the fork, append, and free methods.

claim.statement: vLLM believes future decoding algorithms can be supported by combining the fork, append, and free methods.

children.statement: PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by virtual memory and paging, is proposed to manage KV cache more efficiently.

claim.statement: PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by virtual memory and paging, is proposed to manage KV cache more efficiently.

children.statement: The idea of virtual memory and paging is effective for managing the KV cache in LLM serving because the workload requires dynamic memory allocation (since the output length is not known a priori) and its performance is bound by the GPU memory capacity.
vLLM, built on PagedAttention, achieves near‑zero waste in KV cache memory and allows flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests.

claim.statement: vLLM, built on PagedAttention, achieves near‑zero waste in KV cache memory and allows flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache -> GPU memory page via PagedAttention
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with pageable memory support

children.statement: vLLM achieves 6.1%–9.8% memory saving on parallel sampling for the Alpaca dataset.
vLLM achieves 37.6%–55.2% memory saving on beam search for the Alpaca dataset.
vLLM achieves 16.2%–30.5% memory saving on parallel sampling for the ShareGPT dataset.
vLLM achieves 44.3%–66.3% memory saving on beam search for the ShareGPT dataset.
vLLM increases throughput of popular LLMs by 2–4× while maintaining the same latency as state‑of‑the‑art systems such as FasterTransformer and Orca.
The reserved memory occupies space that could otherwise be used to process other requests, especially when the reserved space is large.
children.rejected_why: occupies space that could be used for other requests

children.statement: The actual effective memory in previous systems can be as low as 20.4% due to these wastes.
Existing LLM serving systems statically allocate a chunk of memory for a request based on the request’s maximum possible sequence length, leading to reserved slots for future tokens that are wasted.
children.rejected_why: wastes memory by reserving slots for future tokens that may never be used

claim_tree.statement: Fine-grained batching mechanisms differ from traditional request‑level batching by operating at the iteration level and eliminating the need to pad inputs and outputs, which reduces queueing delay and inefficiencies, thereby significantly increasing throughput.
These general systems fail to take into account the autoregressive property and token state of LLM inference, resulting in missed opportunities for optimization.

vLLM increases memory utilization and reduces fragmentation, enabling sharing, and runs more requests in a batch, achieving a 2–4× speedup compared to Orca, whereas Orca achieves throughput gains via scheduling and interleaving.

vLLM introduces block‑level memory management for online serving, whereas FlexGen does not target online serving, OLLA does not do fine‑grained block‑level management or online serving, and FlashAttention applies tiling and kernel optimizations but not block‑level memory management.
vLLM is an LLM serving engine built on PagedAttention, featuring a centralized scheduler that coordinates distributed GPU workers.

claim.statement: vLLM is an LLM serving engine built on PagedAttention, featuring a centralized scheduler that coordinates distributed GPU workers.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Centralized scheduling reduces coordination overhead but may limit scalability
universal_facets.analogy_source: Like a traffic controller coordinating cars

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV cache -> physical GPU memory via scheduler
systems_facets.s5_policy: dynamic page allocation guided by scheduler
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: multiple GPUs with interconnect (e.g., NVLink)

children.statement: The design of vLLM facilitates effective memory management for various decoding methods, such as parallel sampling, beam search, and shared prompt generation.

claim.statement: The design of vLLM facilitates effective memory management for various decoding methods, such as parallel sampling, beam search, and shared prompt generation.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Lower memory usage versus increased block management overhead

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV block mapped to physical KV block using reference counts and copy‑on‑write
systems_facets.s5_policy: Reference‑count based sharing with copy‑on‑write and block reclamation when count reaches zero
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with pageable memory

children.statement: For each decoding iteration, vLLM selects candidate sequences for batching, allocates physical blocks, concatenates all input tokens into a single sequence, feeds it into the LLM, and uses the PagedAttention kernel to access previous KV cache stored in logical blocks while storing new KV cache into physical blocks; storing multiple tokens per KV block increases parallelism and reduces latency, though larger block size increases fragmentation.

claim.statement: For each decoding iteration, vLLM selects candidate sequences for batching, allocates physical blocks, concatenates all input tokens into a single sequence, feeds it into the LLM, and uses the PagedAttention kernel to access previous KV cache stored in logical blocks while storing new KV cache into physical blocks; storing multiple tokens per KV block increases parallelism and reduces latency, though larger block size increases fragmentation.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Block size increases parallelism but causes memory fragmentation

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate new physical block when logical block full, fill left-to-right
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with compute capability supporting PagedAttention kernel

children.statement: On the Alpaca trace, block sizes 16 and 32 tokens work well, while larger block sizes significantly degrade performance.
Recomputation overhead remains constant across different block sizes, as it does not utilize KV blocks.
Recomputation is more efficient for small block sizes, swapping is more efficient for large block sizes, and recomputation overhead is never higher than 20% of swapping’s latency.
For medium block sizes from 16 to 64, recomputation and swapping exhibit comparable end-to-end performance.
External fragmentation from the memory allocator (e.g., buddy allocator) results in memory that will never be used for generated tokens.
Compaction has been proposed as a potential solution to fragmentation, but performing compaction in a performance-sensitive LLM serving system is impractical due to the massive KV cache.
children.rejected_why: impractical due to massive KV cache

children.statement: Even with compaction, the pre-allocated chunk space for each request prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms in existing memory management systems.
children.rejected_why: prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms

children.statement: The system design of vLLM operates in a distributed setting, scaling across multiple GPU workers.

claim.statement: The system design of vLLM operates in a distributed setting, scaling across multiple GPU workers.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Scales memory across GPUs, incurring communication overhead
universal_facets.analogy_source: Like a distributed team sharing a common plan

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV blocks -> physical GPU memory blocks via centralized scheduler
systems_facets.s5_policy: per-iteration KV block scheduling by scheduler
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: multiple GPUs with allreduce support

children.statement: PagedAttention partitions the KV cache of each sequence into KV blocks of fixed size B, allowing keys and values to be stored in non‑contiguous memory.

claim.statement: PagedAttention partitions the KV cache of each sequence into KV blocks of fixed size B, allowing keys and values to be stored in non‑contiguous memory.

children.statement: Operating systems techniques such as virtual memory and copy-on-write can be adapted to efficiently manage KV cache and handle various decoding algorithms in LLM serving.
PagedAttention allows attention keys and values to be stored in non-contiguous paged memory.
The attention computation is transformed into a blockwise computation as shown in Eq.4, where the kernel processes each KV block separately.

claim.statement: The attention computation is transformed into a blockwise computation as shown in Eq.4, where the kernel processes each KV block separately.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: More flexible memory usage at cost of additional kernel overhead

children.statement: During prefill, vLLM maps the first logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks and generates the KV cache of the prompt and first output token using conventional self‑attention.

claim.statement: During prefill, vLLM maps the first logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks and generates the KV cache of the prompt and first output token using conventional self‑attention.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Larger block size boosts parallelism and latency, but raises memory fragmentation.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate new physical block when logical block full; map via block table
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with block-based KV cache and PagedAttention kernel

children.statement: vLLM maintains lower normalized latency across request rates compared to Orca variants, achieving a normalized latency of 0.0 at request rates up to 1.0 req/s, whereas Orca (Max) and Orca (Pow2) have normalized latencies of 1.0 and 0.5 respectively.

vLLM manages memory for multiple sequences by mapping each sequence’s logical blocks to distinct physical blocks within the GPU workers’ reserved space; neighboring logical blocks need not be contiguous in physical memory, enabling efficient utilization of physical block space.

claim.statement: vLLM manages memory for multiple sequences by mapping each sequence’s logical blocks to distinct physical blocks within the GPU workers’ reserved space; neighboring logical blocks need not be contiguous in physical memory, enabling efficient utilization of physical block space.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: conventional contiguous memory allocation that reserves maximum sequence length
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Balancing memory fragmentation against efficient GPU memory utilization

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block → physical KV block via block table mapping
systems_facets.s5_policy: allocate new physical block when logical block full and previous blocks full; fill left-to-right
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with memory partitioning into KV blocks

children.statement: vLLM shares the KV cache of the prompt across multiple output samples by mapping the same logical blocks of the prompt to identical physical blocks and maintaining reference counts for each physical block.

claim.statement: vLLM shares the KV cache of the prompt across multiple output samples by mapping the same logical blocks of the prompt to identical physical blocks and maintaining reference counts for each physical block.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory savings vs added cache management overhead

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via reference counting
systems_facets.s5_policy: Share prompt KV blocks across samples; use reference counting to manage block lifetimes
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with sufficient memory to hold KV cache

children.statement: vLLM implements a copy‑on‑write mechanism at block granularity: when a physical block with reference count greater than one needs to be modified by a sequence, vLLM allocates a new physical block, copies the data, and updates reference counts.

claim.statement: vLLM implements a copy‑on‑write mechanism at block granularity: when a physical block with reference count greater than one needs to be modified by a sequence, vLLM allocates a new physical block, copies the data, and updates reference counts.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical block via copy‑on‑write
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: For beam search, vLLM shares KV blocks among beam candidates, frees blocks whose reference counts drop to zero, and allocates new physical blocks for new candidates, thereby reducing memory copy overhead.

claim.statement: For beam search, vLLM shares KV blocks among beam candidates, frees blocks whose reference counts drop to zero, and allocates new physical blocks for new candidates, thereby reducing memory copy overhead.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Lower memory copy overhead, increased bookkeeping overhead
universal_facets.analogy_source: Like sharing a library among readers

children.statement: vLLM improves throughput over Orca (Oracle) on OPT-13B with the Alpaca dataset from 1.3× in basic sampling to 2.3× in beam search with a width of 6.

vLLM achieves a higher average number of batched requests than Orca variants when serving OPT-13B on ShareGPT and Alpaca traces, with 72.75 batched requests on ShareGPT and 43.24 on Alpaca, compared to 7.00, 9.81, and 13.62 for Orca (Max), Orca (Pow2), and Orca (Oracle) on ShareGPT, and 7.00 for Orca (Max) on Alpaca.

vLLM reserves physical blocks for predefined shared prefixes, mapping user prompts with the shared prefix to these cached physical blocks, enabling prompt engineering and reducing redundant computation.

claim.statement: vLLM reserves physical blocks for predefined shared prefixes, mapping user prompts with the shared prefix to these cached physical blocks, enabling prompt engineering and reducing redundant computation.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Increased memory usage for reduced computation

systems_facets.s4_mapping: shared prefix prompt -> cached KV block via mapping
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with KV cache support

children.statement: When a one‑shot prefix (80 tokens) is shared among requests, vLLM achieves 1.67× higher throughput than Orca (Oracle).
When a five‑shot prefix (341 tokens) is shared among requests, vLLM achieves 3.58× higher throughput than Orca (Oracle).
PagedAttention makes vLLM significantly outperform FasterTransformer in end-to-end performance.
On the ShareGPT trace, vLLM achieves best performance when using block sizes between 16 and 128 tokens.
NCCL is used for tensor communication across distributed GPU workers.

claim.statement: NCCL is used for tensor communication across distributed GPU workers.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: tensor -> GPU memory via NCCL
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: NVIDIA GPUs with NCCL support

children.statement: vLLM sustains 2× higher request rates compared to the three Orca baselines on the ShareGPT chatbot workload.
vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving system with efficient memory management enabled by PagedAttention.

claim_tree.statement: vLLM, built on PagedAttention, achieves near‑zero waste in KV cache memory and allows flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests.

claim.statement: vLLM, built on PagedAttention, achieves near‑zero waste in KV cache memory and allows flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache entries mapped to memory pages using paging
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic page allocation and sharing policy for KV cache
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU

children.statement: vLLM increases throughput of popular LLMs by 2–4× while maintaining the same latency as state‑of‑the‑art systems such as FasterTransformer and Orca.
The throughput improvement is more pronounced for longer input sequences.

claim_tree.statement: The design of vLLM handles variable-length input and output sequences, allowing flexible batching across requests.

claim.statement: The design of vLLM handles variable-length input and output sequences, allowing flexible batching across requests.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: sequence group -> GPU KV blocks via all-or-nothing eviction
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory and CPU memory for KV cache swapping

children.statement: vLLM achieves higher throughput for single sequence generation on OPT-13B and OPT-66B models on ShareGPT and Alpaca datasets, with 132.44 requests per second for OPT-13B on ShareGPT, 72.75 on Alpaca, 30.42 requests per second for OPT-66B on ShareGPT, and 43.24 on Alpaca.
The throughput improvement is more pronounced for longer input sequences.

claim_tree.statement: In the first autoregressive decoding step, vLLM generates the new token with the PagedAttention algorithm on the allocated physical blocks and updates the block table’s #filled record.

claim.statement: In the first autoregressive decoding step, vLLM generates the new token with the PagedAttention algorithm on the allocated physical blocks and updates the block table’s #filled record.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Larger block size boosts parallelism and lowers latency but raises memory fragmentation

claim_tree.statement: When a logical block becomes full, vLLM allocates a new physical block, stores the newly generated KV cache there, and records the mapping in the block table.

claim.statement: When a logical block becomes full, vLLM allocates a new physical block, stores the newly generated KV cache there, and records the mapping in the block table.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: pre-allocating KV cache memory for maximum sequence length
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: larger block size improves parallelism but increases fragmentation

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: allocate new physical block when logical block becomes full
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with block engine for KV block allocation

claim_tree.statement: vLLM adopts a first-come-first-serve (FCFS) scheduling policy for all requests, ensuring fairness and preventing starvation.

claim.statement: vLLM adopts a first-come-first-serve (FCFS) scheduling policy for all requests, ensuring fairness and preventing starvation.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence group -> GPU KV cache blocks via all-or-nothing eviction and CPU swap
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with dedicated KV cache memory and CPU RAM for swapping

claim_tree.statement: vLLM implements an all-or-nothing eviction policy, evicting either all or none of the blocks of a sequence.

claim.statement: vLLM implements an all-or-nothing eviction policy, evicting either all or none of the blocks of a sequence.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence -> GPU KV cache blocks via all-or-nothing eviction
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with dedicated memory and CPU RAM for swap

claim_tree.statement: Multiple sequences within one request are gang-scheduled as a sequence group, and sequences within a group are preempted or rescheduled together due to potential memory sharing.

claim.statement: Multiple sequences within one request are gang-scheduled as a sequence group, and sequences within a group are preempted or rescheduled together due to potential memory sharing.

children.statement: Once a sequence is preempted and evicted, vLLM stops accepting new requests until all preempted sequences are completed.

claim.statement: Once a sequence is preempted and evicted, vLLM stops accepting new requests until all preempted sequences are completed.

algorithm_facets.g3: Prevents new requests until all preempted sequences finish
algorithm_facets.g5: List of preempted sequences and their evicted blocks

children.statement: Once a request completes, its blocks are freed, and the blocks of a preempted sequence are brought back to continue processing.

claim.statement: Once a request completes, its blocks are freed, and the blocks of a preempted sequence are brought back to continue processing.

universal_facets.analogy_source: like swapping pages between disk and RAM

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Preempted sequence KV cache blocks -> GPU memory blocks via swap recovery
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction policy (evict entire sequence or none)
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory and CPU RAM available for swapping

children.statement: The number of blocks swapped to CPU RAM never exceeds the number of total physical blocks in GPU RAM, so the swap space on CPU RAM is bounded by the GPU memory allocated for the KV cache.

claim.statement: The number of blocks swapped to CPU RAM never exceeds the number of total physical blocks in GPU RAM, so the swap space on CPU RAM is bounded by the GPU memory allocated for the KV cache.

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory allocated for KV cache blocks
systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache blocks -> GPU memory blocks via swapping to CPU RAM
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction policy for entire sequence groups
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with dedicated KV cache memory and CPU RAM for swap

children.statement: The static pre-allocation scheme causes internal fragmentation due to over-provisioning for potential maximum sequence lengths, which remains unused until the request finishes sampling.

claim_tree.statement: vLLM includes a CPU block allocator to manage physical blocks swapped to CPU RAM.

claim.statement: vLLM includes a CPU block allocator to manage physical blocks swapped to CPU RAM.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: improves GPU memory management by swapping blocks to CPU RAM

systems_facets.s1_resource: CPU RAM blocks swapped from GPU
systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache block (logical) -> CPU RAM block (physical) via swapping
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory and CPU RAM with sufficient bandwidth

claim_tree.statement: When vLLM exhausts free physical blocks, it selects a set of sequences to evict and transfer their KV cache to the CPU.

claim.statement: When vLLM exhausts free physical blocks, it selects a set of sequences to evict and transfer their KV cache to the CPU.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Increased latency due to CPU-GPU transfer versus reduced GPU memory pressure

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence KV cache -> CPU memory block via swapping
systems_facets.s5_policy: Eviction policy selecting sequences to evict when GPU memory exhausted
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with dedicated memory and CPU memory accessible via PCIe

children.statement: Swapping incurs excessive overhead when block sizes are small, due to numerous small data transfers between CPU and GPU limiting PCIe bandwidth.

claim_tree.statement: vLLM recomputes the KV cache when preempted sequences are rescheduled.

claim.statement: vLLM recomputes the KV cache when preempted sequences are rescheduled.

universal_facets.analogy_source: like swapping a book between shelves

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache blocks of a sequence -> GPU memory blocks via recomputation
systems_facets.s5_policy: recomputation strategy for evicted KV cache blocks
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with limited memory and CPU memory for swapping

children.statement: The frontend extends the OpenAI API interface, allowing users to customize sampling parameters for each request, such as the maximum sequence length and beam width k.

systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: NVIDIA GPU with CUDA support

claim_tree.statement: Recomputation latency can be significantly lower than the original latency because tokens generated at decoding can be concatenated with the original prompt as a new prompt, allowing the KV cache at all positions to be generated in one prompt phase iteration.

claim.statement: Recomputation latency can be significantly lower than the original latency because tokens generated at decoding can be concatenated with the original prompt as a new prompt, allowing the KV cache at all positions to be generated in one prompt phase iteration.

negative_claims.statement: Existing LLM serving systems statically allocate a chunk of memory for a request based on the request’s maximum possible sequence length, leading to reserved slots for future tokens that are wasted.
negative_claims.rejected_why: wastes memory by reserving slots for future tokens that may never be used

negative_claims.statement: The static pre-allocation scheme causes internal fragmentation due to over-provisioning for potential maximum sequence lengths, which remains unused until the request finishes sampling.
External fragmentation from the memory allocator (e.g., buddy allocator) results in memory that will never be used for generated tokens.
The reserved memory occupies space that could otherwise be used to process other requests, especially when the reserved space is large.
negative_claims.rejected_why: occupies space that could be used for other requests

negative_claims.statement: The actual effective memory in previous systems can be as low as 20.4% due to these wastes.
Compaction has been proposed as a potential solution to fragmentation, but performing compaction in a performance-sensitive LLM serving system is impractical due to the massive KV cache.
negative_claims.rejected_why: impractical due to massive KV cache

negative_claims.statement: Even with compaction, the pre-allocated chunk space for each request prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms in existing memory management systems.
negative_claims.rejected_why: prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms
